EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward retreating warm front served as the primary focus for convective development. Afternoon heating resulted in destabilization and several storms became severe, with large hail being the primary impact. Isolated flash flooding also occurred. These thunderstorms also had excessive lightning, which resulted in a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported along a swath from near Lewisville to near Pfafftown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking from the Gulf of Mexico northeast and off the southeast and mid atlantic coast produced between two inches and ten inches of snow across northeast North Carolina from late Wednesday night, February 25th through Thursday morning, February 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between one inch and five inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major Arctic blast to affect the region within the same 7-day period surged through the region on the 18th sending temperatures to their lowest levels in over a year and by the morning of the 20th setting record low temperatures.  Maximum temperatures on the 19th failed to rise above 20F across the Piedmont and failed to even reach 10F across the western mountains.  All of the climate stations within the Blacksburg National Weather Service Forecast Office County Warning Area (CWA) tied record low maximum temperatures on the 18th and all but Bluefield did the same on the 20th.  All of the climate stations set record low temperatures the morning of February 20th, with Lynchburg recording a new all time record low temperature of 11F early in the morning on the 20th.  The first morning after the Arctic frontal passage brought bitterly cold temperatures and gusty northwest winds leading to dangerously low wind chills.  Below are the highlights of the dangerously low -20F or lower wind chills from the North Carolina counties within the Blacksburg National Weather Service Forecast area as well as the plethora of record low and record low maximum temperatures set as a result of this Arctic outbreak:||Wind Chills:|Alleghany County - mesonet station at Barrett recorded a wind chill of -26F at 825 am EST and another mesonet station 2S Whiteheat recorded a wind chill of -24F at 837 am EST,|Ashe County - the AWOS at Jefferson recorded a wind chill of -29F at 755 am EST.  Mesonet stations 2ENE Baldwin and 1WSW Laurel Springs recorded wind chills of -24F at 810 am EST and 800 am EST, respectively.|Watauga County - a mesonet station 2WNW Aho recorded a wind chill of -31F at 729 am EST, another mesonet station 1ESE Boone recorded a wind chill of -30F at 835 am EST, and the Boone AWOS (KTNB) recorded a wind chill reading of -20F at 904 am EST.||Record Low Maximum Temperatures on the 19th:|Boone - maximum of 3F tied for the 5th coldest on record and the coldest since 2/5/1996.  The all time coldest is -4F set on 1/21/1985. ||Note:  Boone is not currently a regular climate station within the Blacksburg, Virginia National Weather Service forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station at Barrett recorded a wind chill of -26F at 825 am EST.  Another mesonet station recorded a wind chill of -24F at 837 am EST.

Afternoon heating resulted in destabilization and several storms became severe, with large hail being the primary impact. Isolated flash flooding also occurred. These thunderstorms also had excessive lightning, which resulted in a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail the size of quarters was reported several miles southwest of Cedar Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved south of Eastern NC while arctic high pressure to the north supplied cold air to the region. The low pressure system caused a wintry mix of precipitation across the area throughout the day, with mainly snow as the precipitation broke out during the morning. A warm front lifted northward through the day, causing snow to change to freezing rain and sleet from south to north. Snow totals ranged from 2 to 4 inches for a large area of east central NC, with around 1 inch of sleet and snow to the south, including the Crystal Coast. The precipitation changed to a period of freezing rain and sleet across the area by mid afternoon before ending around mid evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals ranged from 1 to 4 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Immediately on the heels of the intense Arctic outbreak that spread into the region on the 14th and 15th came the most significant snow storm to affect the region since February 12th and 13th of 2014.  The snow storm was the result of a strong upper-level disturbance tracking from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S. on top of the bitterly cold Arctic air mass.  A surface low pressure area tracked across the southeast states to off the North Carolina coast, a fairly typical scenario for bigger snowfall events within the region.  Temperatures had little to no time to recover at all from the bitterly cold temperatures of the 15th.  As snow spread into the region during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 16th, temperatures were only in the upper teens to lower 20s across the region and fell back into the 10 to 20 degree range across much of the region area during the heavier snow. Snowfall amounts were significant in many areas, ranging from 3 to 4 inches across the Piedmont, where some sleet mixed in during the later part of the event, to 8 to 11 inches across the New River Valley, Greenbrier Valley, and Tazewell county in far southwest Virginia.  Here are the snowfall amounts from the northwest and north central North Carolina counties within our forecast area:||Alleghany County - 5.5 to 5.0 inches in the Glade Valley area,|Ashe County - 5.3 inches near Shatley Springs to 3.0 inches 2SSE of Fleetwood,|Caswell County - 3.8 inches 3NE Pelham to 2.0 inches at Yanceyville (1/2 inch of this was sleet),|Rockingham County - 4.0 inches 3ENE Stoneville to 2.0 inches at Monroeton,|Stokes County - 5.0 inches at King to 3.0 inches at Lawsonville,|Surry County - 4.0 inches at Mt. Airy to 3.0 inches at Westfield,|Watauga County - 6.0 inches at Blowing Rock to 3.0 inches at Zionville,|Wilkes County - 4.7 inches at Hays to 3.0 inches near North Wilkesboro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed 5.0 to 5.5 inches of snow in the Glade Valley area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid level wave resulted in isentropic lift over a shallow layer of cold air. This resulted in a brief period of light freezing rain across much of the Piedmont and portions of the Sandhills. Amounts generally ranged from a trace to couple tenths of an ice. The main impact was numerous car accidents as elevated surfaces became icy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Piedmont Triad Airport ASOS reported 0.06 inches of freezing rain and similar amounts were reported across the county from other sources.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds developed during the evening of the 14th across the northern mountains and foothills in the wake of an arctic cold front. Quite a few trees and power lines were reported down in addition to some minor structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area took a fairly classic path from the northeast Gulf to off the North Carolina coast between the afternoon of the 25th and the morning of the 26th.  However, the track of the low was a little further south and east than needed to bring optimal snowfall to the region.  Snowfall amounts were heaviest across the southern counties of the forecast area and especially across the North Carolina counties.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 4.0 to 8.0 inches across northwest and north central North Carolina, to 3.0 to 6.0 inches across southwest Virginia and Southside Virginia, mostly east of the Blue Ridge, to 2.0 to 3.0 inches further north across southeast West Virginia and toward the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia.  The heaviest snow was nearly all south of U.S. 460 across the forecast area.||Here are the snowfall amounts reported from the North Carolina counties within the Blacksburg, Virginia National Weather Service Forecast Office area:||Alleghany County - 7.0 inches at Sparta to 6.0 inches at Ennice,|Ashe County - 8.0 inches at Glendale Springs to 4.8 inches at West Jefferson,|Caswell County - 7.2 inches at Cherry Grove to 5.0 inches at Providence,|Rockingham County - 8.0 inches at Ruffin to 4.0 inches at Eden,|Stokes County - 6.3 inches at Francisco to 4.0 inches at King,|Surry County - 8.0 inches at Ararat to 5.0 inches 2S of Dobson,|Watauga County - 8.0 inches near Aho to 4.5 inches 2W of Boone,|Wilkes County - 7.0 inches 2N of Maple Springs to 3.0 inches 5SE of Windy Gap,|Yadkin County - 6.0 inches at East Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of snowfall ranging from 5.0 to 6.3 inches were received by the public and trained spotters throughout the county.  The highest amount was at Francisco with 6.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front was lifting back to the north in response to a building offshore ridge. Widely scattered thunderstorms developed as instability increased. Wet bulb zeros were also low. One of these thunderstorms produced large hail along the Robeson and Hoke county line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters to near half-dollars was reported at the Parkton Grill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving from the Southern Plains east northeast and off the Mid Atlantic Coast produced between one inch and two inches of snow, plus one quarter inch to one half inch of ice from freezing rain across northeast North Carolina from early Monday evening, February 16th through early Tuesday morning, February 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between one half inch and two inches across the county. Ice amounts ranged between one quarter inch and one half inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold Arctic high pressure was in place over the region as a surface low developed along the southeast coast due to an eastward moving shortwave. All of central North Carolina was under a winter storm warning. Snowfall totals ranged from 1-3 inches across the extreme northwestern Piedmont to a trace across the southeastern portion of the forecast area. Freezing rain also fell across all of the region, ranging from a quarter to a third of an inch across the south and east to just a trace further north and west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts 2 to 4 inches fell across the county. In addition, a trace of freezing rain accrual was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed near the Blue Ridge during the afternoon. A couple of these storms became briefly strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FD reported nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed near the Blue Ridge during the afternoon. A couple of these storms became briefly strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported half dollar size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rain fell near the North Carolina Blue Ridge throughout much of the 19th. Meanwhile, numerous heavy rain showers and a few thunderstorms moved across the Piedmont during the afternoon and evening in association with a warm front. This was after a round of moderate to heavy rain moved across the area early in the day, producing up to 2 inches of rain. Localized flooding developed during the late afternoon and evening near the Blue Ridge, while flash flooding developed across portions of the Charlotte metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported flash flooding developed across the City of Charlotte area after about an inch of rain fell in about an hour associated with an area of thunderstorms. This was on top of 1-2 inches that had already fallen earlier in the day. A small stream flooded units at an apartment complex on Tacoma St (4 WNW City Center) while units at another complex were flooded on Wallace Dr (6.5 SE). Just west of this location, an automated stream gauge on McMullen Creek exceeded its established flood stage, indicating that a portion of Addison Dr was covered in water.

This was on top of 1-2 inches that had already fallen earlier in the day. Affected roads included North Tryon St at Mallard Church Rd and  Pavillion Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold arctic high pressure centered over the Great Lakes, combined with low pressure tracking off the coast of South Carolina produced snow and ice over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began before dawn, accumulating as much as an inch near St. Pauls.  The northern third of the county received around an inch of snowfall, with lesser amounts to the south. The precipitation transitioned to freezing rain by afternoon, with a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold arctic high pressure centered over the Great Lakes, combined with low pressure tracking off the coast of South Carolina produced snow and ice over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began before dawn, accumulating as much as 1.7 northwest of the Elizabethtown.  About two thirds of the county received over an inch of snowfall.  The precipitation transitioned to freezing rain by afternoon, with a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the mountains during early and mid-afternoon. A couple of these produced localized excessive rain, hail and/or wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the mountains during early and mid-afternoon. A couple of these produced localized excessive rain, hail and/or wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FD reported Shawneehaw Creek overflowed its banks and flooded part of Main St in downtown Banner Elk after 2-3 inches of rain fell in the area in just over an hour. In addition, urban flooding was reported throughout the city due to poor drainage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry fuels contributed to a fire that started in the Compass Pointe Division in Brunswick County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry fuels contributed to a fire that started in the Compass Pointe Division in Brunswick County.  The fire started near railroad tracks east of post 354.  The fire burned a total of 465 acres, 400 of which were woodlands.  There were five homes damaged, and two structures destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low and frontal boundary produced impressive rainfall in a 24-hour period ending at 12Z on the 20th. There were widespread 3 to 5 inch amounts with isolated higher totals (6���+ in Alleghany County, NC).  Flood Advisories were issued in the late afternoon of April 19th for a number of northwest NC counties quickly followed by a Flash Flood Warning (FFW) for Watauga and Ashe counties at 625 PM EDT. Flooding was reported at several locations in Watauga County during the afternoon and evening, mainly flooded roads. The Watauga River gage at Sugar Grove gage (SGWN7) crested at 11.37 feet at 02Z on the 20th. Moderate Flood Stage is 10 feet and Minor Flood Stage is 6 feet. The Dan River at Wentworth (WENN7) crested at 19.45 feet (Minor FS = 19 feet).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of roads were closed across the county for much of the day due to flooding from the persistent rainfall. Several low-water crossings on the Watauga River were flooded to a considerable depth including DeWitt Barnett Road and Guy Ford Road. Along the South Fork of the New River a low-water crossing at Roby Green road was flooded. Watauga River Road near Romminger was also closed by flooding from the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front blasted through the western Carolinas during the afternoon and evening of the 18th, bringing strong winds and very cold air to the region. Overnight, sustained winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with air temperatures in the teens to yield wind chill values around 0 by daybreak on the 19th. Although winds diminished, air temperatures failed to warm above the 20s throughout the 19th, while record lows between 0 and 10 above were recorded the morning of the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front blasted through the western Carolinas during the afternoon and evening of the 18th, bringing strong winds and very cold air to the region. Overnight, sustained winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with air temperatures in the teens to yield wind chill values around 0 by daybreak on the 19th. Although winds diminished, air temperatures failed to warm above the 20s throughout the 19th, while record lows between 0 and 10 above were recorded the morning of the 20th.

Very cold Arctic high pressure was in place over the region as a surface low developed along the southeast coast due to an eastward moving shortwave. All of central North Carolina was under a winter storm warning. Snowfall totals ranged from 1-3 inches across the extreme northwestern Piedmont to a trace across the southeastern portion of the forecast area. Freezing rain also fell across all of the region, ranging from a quarter to a third of an inch across the south and east to just a trace further north and west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around an inch fell across the county. In addition, a tenth to a quarter of an inch of freezing rain accrual was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled cold front during the morning lifted northward in the afternoon. Several rounds of storms develop in the vicinity of the front. Some of the storms became severe and produced severe hail and damaging winds, along with localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down across the county with the worst of the damage near Buies Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed as an unseasonably strong mid level trough approached the region. With a prefrontal trough serving as a low level focus and a very moist and unstable air mass in place, many of the storms became severe, producing wind damage. In addition, a brief tornado spun up in Lee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down along a swath from several miles south of Turkey to near Ivanhoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled cold front during the morning lifted northward in the afternoon. Several rounds of storms develop in the vicinity of the front. Some of the storms became severe and produced severe hail and damaging winds, along with localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down onto a home. No further information was available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled cold front during the morning lifted northward in the afternoon. Several rounds of storms develop in the vicinity of the front. Some of the storms became severe and produced severe hail and damaging winds, along with localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large Gum tree was blown down across Bill Shaw Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent upper trough over the region was amplified as a strong vorticity maximum approached and crossed the region through the evening hours. With sufficient moisture, instability and shear, thunderstorms developed and organized into a broken line, which crossed most of the forecast area and produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent upper trough over the region was amplified as a strong vorticity maximum approached and crossed the region through the evening hours. With sufficient moisture, instability and shear, thunderstorms developed and organized into a broken line, which crossed most of the forecast area and produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 60 mph was measured at the Piedmont Triad International Airport. In addition the emergency manager reported that numerous trees and power lines were blown down. A couple of the trees fell on top of homes. Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid level shear axis interacted with a moist and unstable air mass, triggering thunderstorms across the Coastal Plain and Sandhills. Slow moving and training storms resulted in flash flooding. In addition, one storm produced some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several Roads were closed on the southern side of Laurinburg due to heavy rain. Some of the roads included, Plaza Road, Kinston Street, Main Street, Raleigh Street and College Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a wedge of cold air spread south across the eastern seaboard, rain changed to freezing rain during the early morning hours of the 1st across the Blue Ridge of North Carolina, as well as the I-40 corridor through the Piedmont and foothills. Ice accretion was light, generally only a few hundredths of an inch, although some locations saw as much as a tenth of an inch. Icing was primarily confined to elevated surfaces, so travel was not impacted significantly.

Low pressure moved east across eastern North Carolina along a frontal boundary during the afternoon of May 21st 2015. Scattered thunderstorms developed over the area with a couple becoming severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor damage to two homes with trees blown down on Wards Bridge Road near Warsaw.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid level wave resulted in isentropic lift over a shallow layer of cold air. This resulted in a brief period of light freezing rain across much of the Piedmont and portions of the Sandhills. Amounts generally ranged from a trace to couple tenths of an ice. The main impact was numerous car accidents as elevated surfaces became icy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Burlington Airport ASOS reported 0.05 inches of freezing rain and similar amounts were reported from other sources across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east across the southeast US, caused a low pressure system to develop off the southeast coast. With very cold air in place over the region, as this low tracked northeast, snow developed across the area. With a winter weather advisory in place, most of central North Carolina received 1-2 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 1 to 2.5 inches fell across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 0.5 to 1 inch fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and Isolated thunderstorms developed along a cold front over the northern parts of eastern North Carolina during the late afternoon and evening of March 11th 2015. A few of the storms became severe producing large hail and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid level wave resulted in isentropic lift over a shallow layer of cold air. This resulted in a brief period of light freezing rain across much of the Piedmont and portions of the Sandhills. Amounts generally ranged from a trace to couple tenths of an ice. The main impact was numerous car accidents as elevated surfaces became icy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported 0.06 inches of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a front draped across the area, an MCV tracked over the region. Given the unstable air mass in place, convection developed and several of the storms became severe. The main impact was very large hail up to the of hen eggs (around 2 inches). One of the storms did significant damage in Franklin county, with holes being put in several homes siding and windows and multiple car windshields being scattered.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail the size of quarters was reported several miles northwest of Oxford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms developing ahead of a cold front moved across the southern Piedmont of North Carolina during the morning, producing gusty winds that caused isolated wind damage. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported hail larger than half dollars near Earl.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported 3/4 inch hail at Williamsburg Ct and Somerset Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and isolated thunderstorms lifted northeast across eastern North Carolina well ahead of a cold front during the late morning of March 26th, 2015. One of the storms produced damaging winds across parts of Greene and Pitt Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes received moderate to substantial damage. One out building with no foundation support was destroyed. Several trees were snapped in half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed across eastern North Carolina during the evening of April 8th, 2015 as a slow moving cold front moved into the area. A couple of the storms produced some sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of nickle size hail near Jackson Heights southwest of Kinston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking from the Gulf of Mexico northeast and off the southeast and mid atlantic coast produced between two inches and ten inches of snow across northeast North Carolina from late Wednesday night, February 25th through Thursday morning, February 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between two inches and four inches across the county. Elizabeth City reported 3.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking from the Gulf of Mexico northeast and off the southeast and mid atlantic coast produced between two inches and ten inches of snow across northeast North Carolina from late Wednesday night, February 25th through Thursday morning, February 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between two inches and four inches across the county. Peach (1 W) reported 3.7 inches of snow. Burgess (2 SW) reported 2.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving east across the southeast US, caused a low pressure system to develop off the southeast coast. With very cold air in place over the region, as this low tracked northeast, snow developed across the area. With a winter weather advisory in place, most of central North Carolina received 1-2 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds with gusts between 35 and 40 mph impacted Rockingham County on April 22nd.  The next day, winds were not quite as strong with gusts in the 25 to 30 mph range. While not overly impressive gusts, the combination of the gusts on the 22nd and the 23rd, combined with recent rains softening the ground a few days prior on the 20th, allowed for the circumstance for a large tree to be blown down onto a house in Wentworth at the intersection of Wentworth Street and Rain Loop. A husband and wife were at home at the time the tree fell and split the house in two. The female sustained minor injuries, bruises. The house was condemned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds with gusts between 35 and 40 mph impacted Rockingham County on April 22nd. The next day, winds were not quite as strong with gusts in the 25 to 30 mph range. While not overly impressive gusts, the combination of the gusts on the 22nd and the 23rd, combined with recent rains softening the ground a few days prior on the 20th, allowed for the circumstance for a large tree to be blown down onto a house in Wentworth at the intersection of Wentworth Street and Rain Loop. A husband and wife were at home at the time the tree fell and split the house in two. The female sustained minor injuries, bruises. The house was condemned. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Ana made landfall along the northeast coast of South Carolina and then tracked northeastward across eastern North Carolina, producing some thunderstorm wind damage in Wilson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weak structures were damaged, one mobile home was blown over and a tree fell on top of another mobile home. In addition, a couple of trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold Arctic high pressure was in place over the region as a surface low developed along the southeast coast due to an eastward moving shortwave. All of central North Carolina was under a winter storm warning. Snowfall totals ranged from 1-3 inches across the extreme northwestern Piedmont to a trace across the southeastern portion of the forecast area. Freezing rain also fell across all of the region, ranging from a quarter to a third of an inch across the south and east to just a trace further north and west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell across the county. In addition, a trace of freezing rain accrual was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough served as a focus for convective development during the late afternoon and evening hours. Given the very moist and unstable air mass, many of the storms became severe, producing wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down in Carrboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough served as a focus for convective development during the late afternoon and evening hours. Given the very moist and unstable air mass, many of the storms became severe, producing wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down along a swath that began several miles east of Chapel Hill and continued to Bethesda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed as an unseasonably strong mid level trough approached the region. With a prefrontal trough serving as a low level focus and a very moist and unstable air mass in place, many of the storms became severe, producing wind damage. In addition, a brief tornado spun up in Lee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down along a swath from a couple miles west of Hasty to several miles southeast of Hasty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed as an unseasonably strong mid level trough approached the region. With a prefrontal trough serving as a low level focus and a very moist and unstable air mass in place, many of the storms became severe, producing wind damage. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Raleigh survey team found that the tornado touched down on the east bank of the Deep River. Hardwood and softwood tree damage indicated wind speeds of 105 mph. The tornado then tracked east and resulted in damage to a home. The damage consisted of roof and shingle damage, siding damage, garden damage, windows blown out of a van and a trampoline that was mangled and blown 130 yards downstream. Maximum winds at this location were estimated to be 90 mph. The tornado then lifted, but while doing so, resulted in several trees being blown down along Rosser Road near Cotten Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed as an unseasonably strong mid level trough approached the region. With a prefrontal trough serving as a low level focus and a very moist and unstable air mass in place, many of the storms became severe, producing wind damage. In addition, a brief tornado spun up in Lee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down just south of Nahunta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With an closed upper low centered over the Tennessee Valley, several disturbances rotated over the region. With plenty of available moisture available, thunderstorms developed during peak heating. Several of these storms became severe, resulting in numerous downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down on Nursery Road several miles northwest of Spring Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid level shear axis interacted with a moist and unstable air mass, triggering thunderstorms across the Coastal Plain and Sandhills. Slow moving and training storms resulted in flash flooding. In addition, one storm produced some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down along a swath from a few miles northwest of Hope Mills to one mile west of Hope Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a wedge of cold air spread south across the eastern seaboard, rain changed to freezing rain during the early morning hours of the 1st across the Blue Ridge of North Carolina, as well as the I-40 corridor through the Piedmont and foothills. Ice accretion was light, generally only a few hundredths of an inch, although some locations saw as much as a tenth of an inch. Icing was primarily confined to elevated surfaces, so travel was not impacted significantly.

Low pressure moved east across eastern North Carolina along a frontal boundary during the afternoon of May 21st 2015. Scattered thunderstorms developed over the area with a couple becoming severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle sized hail reported 7 miles east-southeast of Grimesland by a spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved east across eastern North Carolina along a frontal boundary during the afternoon of May 21st 2015. Scattered thunderstorms developed over the area with a couple becoming severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported by a spotter along Highway 17 in Vanceboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered clusters of thunderstorms developed across much of western North Carolina during early afternoon and continued into the evening hours. A couple of storms produced brief severe weather in the form of damaging winds, while a cluster of storms produced significant flash flooding in southern portions of the Charlotte metro area for the second evening in a row.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FD reported a tree and power lines blown down on a garage off Wilkesboro Highway and multiple trees and power lines blown down in the area around the I-77 and Highway 21 intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered clusters of thunderstorms developed across much of western North Carolina during early afternoon and continued into the evening hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding developed for the second night in a row across northwest Union County, after 2 to 3 inches of rain fell in a short period of time on top of soil saturated from the 3 to 6 inches that fell on the evening of the 18th. In addition to urban flooding issues, the main stream affected was Goose Creek, where an official NWS stream on Highway 1524 far exceeded the estimated flood stage. In fact, the 13 feet reading was a record for this site. Fire dept reported multiple water rescues on flooded roads that included Red Lanter Rd, Briarwood Ct, Redwood Rd, and Indian Trail Fairview Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain ended in the Goose Creek basin by mid-evening of the 19th, continued runoff resulted in the stream remaining out of its banks and flooding several roads, including the intersection of Highways 218 and 601, until well after midnight.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: FD reported water flooded the basement of a home on Saddlewood Circle after about 2 inches of rain fell in a short period of time. The flooding was described as being the result of poor drainage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across the mountains during the evening. One slow-moving storm developed over the Weaverville area and produced flash flooding in the Weaverville area while another produced brief damaging winds in east Asheville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported multiple trees and power lines blown down on Chunns Cove Rd near Tunnel Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across the mountains during the evening. One slow-moving storm developed over the Weaverville area and produced flash flooding in the Weaverville area while another produced brief damaging winds in east Asheville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms and FD reported localized flash flooding developed in the Weaverville area after 2 to 4 inches of rain fell in a short period of time. Several small creeks overflowed their banks and flooded roads as well as the basements of some homes on Woodland Hills Dr, Hamburg Mountain Rd, and Lakeshore Dr. In addition, water from a stream along Merrimon Rd resulted in stalling of a vehicle, with the driver requiring rescue. At least one small mudslide also occurred in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep amplitude east coast upper trough closed off across the southeast states for several days while a surface front meandered across the area. Deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean continued to feed into the area. Tropical moisture from the periphery of Hurricane Joaquin, well off the coast, helped to boost precipitable water values to near record levels. Rainfall amounts averaged 5 to 15 inches across the area during the event. Isolated spots received near 20 inches. This produced rare flash flooding. Coastal flooding was significant during the time of high tide. Widespread river flooding followed. There were more than a hundred evacuations due to flooding, but no reports of death or injury. There was significant damage to structures to include homes and businesses and infrastructure such as roads as well as personal property. There were reports of overtopped levees and earthen dams. Monetary losses were estimated to be several hundred thousand dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dorsay Lane was flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near Greensboro, near the intersection of Pinecroft Road and W Meadowview Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An earthen dam at Sunset Beach was overtopped. Another earthen dam on Heather Lake, near the state line, was overtopped as well. Flooding was characterized by officials on the ground as widespread as up to a foot of rain fell across the area during the afternoon and evening. Multiple homes were reportedly flooded up to knee deep in the Spring Mill Plantation and Carolina Shores communities in Calabash. Numerous families were evacuated from their homes and many were taken to area shelters. Twenty vehicles were stranded in flood waters. The occupants of these vehicles had to be rescued and brought to safety due to the rapidity of the water rise.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving area of upper level low pressure tapped into a tropical moisture feed associated with Hurricane Joaquin to produce a band of moderate to heavy rainfall across the western Carolinas during the morning hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening upper trough and surface cold front approached the area, triggering scattered storms within a very moist air mass. A few of the slow moving storms produced 3 to 4 inches around the Norwood area in Stanly County, resulting in minor flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a strong upper low nearly stationary over the Florida Gulf Coast, a steady moisture feed resulted in a very moist air mass over the region. An old front was aligned along the Carolina coast and isentropic lift over this boundary produced a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain. All of central North Carolina received 3-6 inches of rain during this time frame. Given the saturated soils, some locations experienced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported near Little Carr Creek, with Yates Hill Road closed west of Route 74 due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual rains on already saturated soil caused some flooding in the Town Creek area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of water covering portions of Hwy 17 near Zion Church Road in the Town Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall occurred overnight up to 12Z (8 AM) on October 1st across parts of northwest North Carolina with 1 to 2 inches falling in Watauga and Wilkes counties. The Dan River in North Carolina (parts of Stokes, Rockingham and Caswell counties) was already past crest on the 1st from the late September rains and stages along the river fell back close to or just below flood stage through the day. Additional rains overspread much of the remainder of the northwest North Carolina during the day on the 1st with widespread light moderate amounts. Precipitation totals were in the 0.25��� to 0.75��� range ending at 12Z on the 2nd although a few sites received over an inch including Eden COOP (EDEN7) 1.10���. The basic pattern was unchanged the following 24 hours with the strong ridge remaining over the northeast and surface low pressure moving slowly along the southeast coast. Bands of moderate to heavy rainfall tracked to the northwest of the surface low into parts of the piedmont with heavier rain was confined mostly south and east of the Blue Ridge divide.   A Flash Flood Warning (FFW) was issued in the early morning hours of the 3rd for western Stokes County, NC as radar indicated 1 to 1.5 inches falling in several hours.  One road was reportedly closed by small stream flood runoff from this event. 24-hour rain totals ending at 12Z on the 3rd ranged generally from 1 to 3 inches with theheaviest across the piedmont counties. The top five raion gage amounts ending at 12Z on the 3rd included King COOP (KIGN7) 3.10���, Eden COOP (EDEN7) 3.06���, and Dan River at Pine Hall (PNHN7) 2.58���, Caswell Game Lands (CGLN7) 2.53 and Danbury COOP 2.31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Goff Road, just northwest of King, was closed due to a sharp rise of water along Danbury Creek.

There was significant damage to structures to include homes and businesses and infrastructure such as roads as well as personal property. There were reports of overtopped levees and earthen dams. Monetary losses were estimated to be several hundred thousand dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 17 was reportedly closed due to flooding near the state line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall occurred overnight up to 12Z (8 AM) on October 1st across parts of northwest North Carolina with 1 to 2 inches falling in Watauga and Wilkes counties. The Dan River in North Carolina (parts of Stokes, Rockingham and Caswell counties) was already past crest on the 1st from the late September rains and stages along the river fell back close to or just below flood stage through the day. Additional rains overspread much of the remainder of the northwest North Carolina during the day on the 1st with widespread light moderate amounts. Precipitation totals were in the 0.25��� to 0.75��� range ending at 12Z on the 2nd although a few sites received over an inch including Eden COOP (EDEN7) 1.10���. The basic pattern was unchanged the following 24 hours with the strong ridge remaining over the northeast and surface low pressure moving slowly along the southeast coast. Bands of moderate to heavy rainfall tracked to the northwest of the surface low into parts of the piedmont with heavier rain was confined mostly south and east of the Blue Ridge divide.   A Flash Flood Warning (FFW) was issued in the early morning hours of the 3rd for western Stokes County, NC as radar indicated 1 to 1.5 inches falling in several hours.  One road was reportedly closed by small stream flood runoff from this event. 24-hour rain totals ending at 12Z on the 3rd ranged generally from 1 to 3 inches with theheaviest across the piedmont counties. The top five raion gage amounts ending at 12Z on the 3rd included King COOP (KIGN7) 3.10���, Eden COOP (EDEN7) 3.06���, and Dan River at Pine Hall (PNHN7) 2.58���, Caswell Game Lands (CGLN7) 2.53 and Danbury COOP 2.31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first report of flooding was 10/3 at 7:19 AM on Barry Hill Bridge. Dan Valley Road and Water Street flooded at 4:54 PM on 10/3. A swift water rescue was performed on Wolf Island Road at 6:30 AM on Saturday 10/3 to extract trapped person in vehicle. Water was reported over Springwood Drive and Country Club Road at 4:21 AM on Sunday 10/4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep amplitude east coast upper trough closed off across the southeast states for several days while a surface front meandered across the area. Deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean continued to feed into the area. Tropical moisture from the periphery of Hurricane Joaquin, well off the coast, helped to boost precipitable water values to near record levels. Rainfall amounts averaged 5 to 15 inches across the area during the event. Isolated spots received near 20 inches. This produced rare flash flooding. Coastal flooding was significant during the time of high tide. Widespread river flooding followed. There were more than a hundred evacuations due to flooding, but no reports of death or injury. There was significant damage to structures to include homes and businesses and infrastructure such as roads as well as personal property. There were reports of overtopped levees and earthen dams. Monetary losses were estimated to be several hundred thousand dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 10 inches of water covered one lane of Middle Sound Loop Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper low over the Tennessee Valley helped to maintain strong easterly winds into the region, along with bouts of moderate to heavy rainfall totaling one to three inches over north central North Carolina and the Foothills. These totals occurred soon after very heavy rain concluded the month of September across a similar location. With a saturated soil, wind gusts of only 25 to 35 mph were enough to bring a large number of trees down across parts of this region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with saturated soils allowed for ten trees to be blown down. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a strong upper low nearly stationary over the Florida Gulf Coast, a steady moisture feed resulted in a very moist air mass over the region. An old front was aligned along the Carolina coast and isentropic lift over this boundary produced a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain. All of central North Carolina received 3-6 inches of rain during this time frame. Given the saturated soils, some locations experienced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty inches of water was reported flowing across South Broad Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near Thomasville near the intersection of Stemp-Everhart Road and Shell Road and was blocking one lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was deepening across the Great Lakes and as a result was pushing a fairly strong cold front for mid-August into the region from the west.  A warm, very moist air mass was present across the region in advance of the front.  As the front moved into the region during afternoon peak heating, scattered to numerous thunderstorms formed across the region.  Rainfall was widespread and some locations received well over an inch of rainfall during the evening.  While a number of the thunderstorms became strong and a tree limb was blown down here and there, only one thunderstorm actually reached severe limits in Surry county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Surry County Emergency Management Director reported that trees were down along Witt Street, Farmbrook Road, and Hamburg Street in Mount Airy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper trough continued to deepen across the Great Lakes.  The associated cold front continued to move east through the region.  A warm, moist air mass remained present in advance of the front.  The front was just west of the Blue Ridge at midday.  As the front moved into the warm, unstable air mass, thunderstorms quickly developed near the Blue Ridge, quickly moving eastward into the Piedmont, especially across Caswell county.  A few of these thunderstorms became strong to severe producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Caswell County 911 Center reported that a large tree was down on Stoney Creek Road approximately five miles northeast of Camp Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving storms produced flooding in Elizabethtown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported by the Knights Inn at 609 East Broad Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was highly unstable with high mixed layer CAPE values and steep lapse rates. The environment was also characterized by high precipitable water values. Developing thunderstorms were able to utilize these ingredients, resulting in pulse severe thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near stationary cold front lied near the Virginia/North Carolina border as did a broad upper trough across the eastern Great Lakes.  Numerous embedded disturbances were evident within the base of the upper trough.  These disturbances continued to impinge on a moist, unstable air mass and produce scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  With the front being located slightly south of its position the previous day, the strong to severe thunderstorms this day were also located a little further south, mainly across northwest and north central North Carolina as opposed to south central Virginia the previous day.  Severe thunderstorms from Watauga to Stokes during the evening blew down a number of trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near stationary cold front lied near the Virginia/North Carolina border as did a broad upper trough across the eastern Great Lakes.  Numerous embedded disturbances were evident within the base of the upper trough.  These disturbances continued to impinge on a moist, unstable air mass and produce scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  With the front being located slightly south of its position the previous day, the strong to severe thunderstorms this day were also located a little further south, mainly across northwest and north central North Carolina as opposed to south central Virginia the previous day.  Severe thunderstorms from Watauga to Stokes during the evening blew down a number of trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Watauga County 911 Center reported that a tree was down at the intersection of State Route 194 and Broadstone Road, blocking the northbound lane of Route 194.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous storms developed when a potent shortwave interacted with a moisture rich environment during peak heating. One storm produced widespread wind damage in Randolph County as numerous trees were blown down. In addition, isolated flash flooding occurred in a couple locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread trees and power lines were blown down along a swath from Ulah to near Coleridge. This included numerous blown down trees at the North Carolina Zoological Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was highly unstable with high mixed layer CAPE values and steep lapse rates. The environment was also characterized by high precipitable water values. Developing thunderstorms were able to utilize these ingredients, resulting in pulse severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reportedly down on Industrial Boulevard. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low was located across eastern Canada with a trough extending southward across the Appalachians.  A nearly stationary cold front was draped west to east across the region near the Virginia/North Carolina border.  Moisture values were quite high across the region.  Thus with ample heating and instability, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed mainly east of the Blue Ridge.  While most of the thunderstorms were on the Virginia side of the state line, the first severe thunderstorm of the afternoon developed in North Carolina in Stokes county causing damage to a number of trees along U.S. 52 just east of King.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A captain from the Stokes County Fire Department reported that multiple trees were down in the White Road and West Dalton road areas of King.  Reports from the public directly, and also via social media outlets, confirmed that numerous trees were down one mile east to one mile north-northeast of King.  While many of the trees were down in one of the city's parks, some trees were down on homes causing damage to the structures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Henderson AWOS reported a 61 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak disturbances aloft interacted with a very moist air mass and triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms. One of these storms resulted in some minor wind damage. Also, a lightning strike injured 18 soldiers during a training activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across the road near Ross Road and Bunnlevel Erwin Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability south of a stationary front and an approaching upper level disturbance helped to develop a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper trough continued to deepen across the Great Lakes.  The associated cold front continued to move east through the region.  A warm, moist air mass remained present in advance of the front.  The front was just west of the Blue Ridge at midday.  As the front moved into the warm, unstable air mass, thunderstorms quickly developed near the Blue Ridge, quickly moving eastward into the Piedmont, especially across Caswell county.  A few of these thunderstorms became strong to severe producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Television Station WGHP reported that several trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds just north of Ruffin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward retreating warm front served as the primary focus for convective development. Afternoon heating resulted in destabilization and several storms became severe, with large hail being the primary impact. Isolated flash flooding also occurred. These thunderstorms also had excessive lightning, which resulted in a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 28 year old male was struck and killed by lightning in the parking lot of a shopping center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over Upstate South Carolina during mid-afternoon and organized into clusters as they moved into the North Carolina Piedmont during late afternoon and evening. These clusters produced strong to severe wind gusts across the southern Piedmont. A falling tree resulted in a fatality near Denver in Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported a 43-year-old female postal worker died when gusty thunderstorm winds blew a large tree down on her vehicle on Norman Ct.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked along a stalled weak frontal boundary across southern portions of central North Carolina and produced scattered showers and storms. a few of the storms became severe and produced damaging winds and quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and power lines were reported down near Patterson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled cold front during the morning lifted northward in the afternoon. Several rounds of storms develop in the vicinity of the front. Some of the storms became severe and produced severe hail and damaging winds, along with localized flash flooding.

The combination of Bermuda high pressure and an upper ridge created hot temperatures and a very unstable environment. Convection initiated along the Piedmont trough and the seabreeze with additional thunderstorms developing along outflow boundaries. A mid-level shortwave ahead of Tropical Storm Bill moved into the western Carolinas, enhancing the lift across southeast North Carolina and resulting in severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of metal roofing was blown off a car dealership. The roofing materials then landed on some of the cars in the lot and damaged them. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms developing ahead of a cold front moved across the southern Piedmont of North Carolina during the morning, producing gusty winds that caused isolated wind damage. After a lull of a few hours, more numerous storms developed during the heat of the afternoon, producing more widespread severe weather in the form of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple spotters and the public reported quarter to golf ball size hail in the Huntersville area, mainly across the west and north side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front produced flooding in the city of Wilmington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Racine Drive near New Centre and Eastwood Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed as a cold front approached and crossed the area during the late afternoon/early evening hours. Although the best upper support remained north of the region, marginal instability and fairly steep lapse rates allowed one of the storms to become severe, producing large hail and some isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported near Rosemary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave and associated low pressure system crossed the region during peak heating resulting is scattered thunderstorms. A few of these storms became strong to severe, producing some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Roseboro.

In addition, a brief tornado spun up in Lee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were blown down in Lucama.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave energy tracking atop a moist low-level maritime feed off the Atlantic Ocean supported moderate to heavy rain over the northwest Piedmont of North Carolina during the morning hours on th 29th. With soil already saturated from prior rainfall, an additional 2.5 inches in just over one hour flooded numerous streets in the city of High Point. Police officers and emergency responders had to rescue several people from cars trapped in high water, but no one was seriously injured. The fire department also reported several calls from residents with flooded basements.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed due to flooding, which includes Elm, Lindsay, Gatewood, Ferndale, Chestnut, Green, Orlando and Ray. Emergency responders performed several water rescues. Water also entered into the basement of several homes. Monetary value of property damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a couple of days of occasional moderate to heavy rain acted to elevate stream flows and soil moisture levels, heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developing along and ahead of a cold front produced 2-3 inches of rain in just a few hours across portions of the Piedmont, resulting in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol, spotter, and media reported flash flooding developed along Beason Creek on the west side of Kings Mountain after as much as 3 inches of rain fell in just a few hours. A bridge on Brook Forest Dr near El Bethel Rd was completely washed out. The creek was also reportedly flooding Phifer Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developing along the Gulf Coast spread very moist air and rain into the western Piedmont early on the 1st, and continuing through much of the 2nd and the early part of the 3rd. Three-day rainfall totals of 2.5 to 4 inches resulted in some flooding across portions of the Charlotte Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms and stream gauges reported flooding developed across mainly the southern part of the county, after about 3 inches of rain fell in a 36-hour period, with most of that falling during the morning of the 2nd. Water from the Rocky River backed into Back Creek, flooding Stallings Rd, Pharr Mill Rd, and Rocky River Rd in Harrisburg. Other closed roads included Bethel Ave Exd, Hopewell Church Rd, and Pine Bluff Rd, all in Midland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up along the East Coast produced rainfall amounts between 1.0 inch and 2.5 inches across much of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts generally ranged between 0.5 inch and 1.5 inches across the county. Burnt Mills (3 SSW) reported 1.39 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a deep trough to our west, several shortwaves moved over the area, triggering showers. With a very moist air mass in place, the heavy rain caused some isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water caused Swepsonville Road to be closed near Bob Vaughn Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developing along a front across the Southeast coast spread moisture and rain into the southern Piedmont of North Carolina throughout the 9th. The rain was moderate to heavy at times, with two inches or more accumulating by the evening. With stream levels already running high due to recent wet weather, minor flooding developed across portions of Cabarrus County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After more than two inches of rain fell across much of Cabarrus County in about 24 hours, a stream gauge on the Rocky River near Irish Buffalo Creek exceeded the established flood stage, eventually by more than a foot. This indicated water backing into Back Creek from the Rocky River was causing flooding of Stallings Rd and Pharr Mill Rd, and that the Rocky River was likely flooding Poplar Tent Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture overspreading a warm front resulted in rain falling across the western Carolinas through much of the 18th into the early 19th. The rain became heavy at times during the afternoon and evening of the 18th. Rainfall totals for the event exceeded 3 inches across the southern mountains, and 2 inches across portions of the southern Piedmont (with locally higher amounts). The heavy rain, combined with high stream flow resulting from recent wet weather, resulted in minor flooding in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After as much as three inches of rain fell over central Henderson County in 24 hours, a stream gauge on Mud Creek near downtown Hendersonville exceeded its established flood stage. This indicated several roads flooded near the intersection of Spartanburg Highway and S Main St.

A portion of Cascade Lake Road was flooded by the Little River near Penrose, while some roads were flooded by the French Broad in the Rosman area, including Main St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture ahead of an approaching cold front resulted in flooding rainfall across southern portions of Eastern NC primarily in Carteret County where 4-8 inches of rainfall occurred in a short period of time in the afternoon. This resulted in flooding of many roads across the county with 2-3 feet of water in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Carteret County Emergency Services received widespread reports of flooding across the county with several roadways covered with water and multiple motor vehicle collisions. Several roadways were impassible during the event. The Emergency Communications Center began receiving calls in reference to flooding at approximately 3:10 pm. Here are some of the locations where flooding occurred. In Atlantic Beach, the intersection Atlantic Beach Causeway and Fort Macon Road was flooded. In Beaufort, the 500 block of Front Street was closed due to flooding, Cedar Street flooded, Pelican Harbor Road/Hwy 70 was flooded, an area of Beaufort Middle School was flooded in front of school and reported water over the hood of a truck, an area of Beaufort Elementary School was flooded,  and Meeting Street was flooded. In Cape Carteret, the intersection of WB Mclean Drive and Golfin Dolphin Drive was flooded. In Emerald Isle, an area of 1201 Ocean Drive yard was flooded, and an area of 124 Doe Drive was flooded. In Newport, 1009 Patch Drive had high water reported, and the 300-466 Block of Broad Creek Loop Rd. was flooded and the road was closed from 456 Broad Creek Loop Rd on the West end to Jones Landing Road on the East end with 2 to 3 �� feet of water on the roadway, Nicks Ave. and Christopher Lane had high water, Bogue Loop Road was flooded, Hibbs Acres Subdivision access off James Drive was impassible, and McCabe Road was flooded. In Morehead City, 15th Street and Bridges Street was blocked and flooded, and John Platt Drive and Penny Lane was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was wedged against the eastern face of the Appalachians, while a deep low pressure system passed across the central Plains states.  The tight pressure gradient between the two features resulted in 60 to 65 knot southeasterly low level jet  along the central Appalachians.  Winds were particularly strong on the west side of the actual ridge lines, where downslope flow acted to draw the winds of the low level jet toward the surface, resulting in surface gusts around 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southeasterly winds resulted in isolated reports of downed trees in Watauga County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a deep trough to our west, several shortwaves moved over the area, triggering showers. With a very moist air mass in place, the heavy rain caused some isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused creeks and streams to leave their banks and flood a half dozen rural roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another complex area of low pressure tracked from the Gulf of Mexico to the Virginia coast November 9-10, bringing a swath of moderate to heavy rainfall across much of the area, but heaviest over the southern sections which were already fairly wet from rains the previous week. Precipitation was highest across parts of northwest North Carolina especially in Watauga County; where six-hour rainfall amounts from 12Z to 18Z on the 9th approached 3 inches in some areas. A Flood Advisory (FLS) was issued for Watauga County as several stream gages approached or exceeded flood stage and several of the normally flood prone areas of the county saw high water and road closures.  Several low water bridges along the Watauga River were under water by 200 pm and the river gage at Sugar Grove (SGWN7) crested at 9.84 feet just below the Moderate Flood Stage of 10 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were reported closed with flooding reported at Boone Mall parking lot, sections of Deerfield Road, Bamboo Road and a section of Roby Greene Road as well as several roads close to the Watauga River.

A stream gauge on Mallard Creek near Pavilion Blvd exceeded its established fllod stage, indicating flooding of Pavilion Blvd near the creek. DOT also reported University City Blvd was closed bewteen Tryon St and Ikea Blvd due to severe drainage flooding.

Two families were evacuated from a mobile home park off Archie Whitesides Rd when Crowders Creek overflowed its banks. McIlwaine Rd was closed due to flooding of a McDowell Creek tributary. Significant road flooding and a house surrounded by water were reported on Gilead Rd, possibly due to a tributary of Torrence Creek overflowing its banks. Significant urban/poor drainage flooding was reported at Highway 115 and Alexandria Rd. A stream gauge on Mallard Creek along Pavillion Blvd exceeded established flood stage by 2 feet, indicating flooding of low lying areas between I-485 and Pavillion Blvd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources reported flash flooding, some of which was significant, developed across much of central and western Cabarrus County after as much as 3 inches of rain fell in just a few hours. Significant drainage  flooding was reported in all urbanized areas, including Oakwood Ave in Kannapolis, multiple streets in Concord, and Old Salisbury-Concord Rd, which was closed in two places due to washed out culverts. Significant stream flooding also developed along the Rocky River and especially its tributaries. A stream gauge on the river above Irish Buffalo Creek exceeded established flood stage by as much as 6 feet.  Roads affected by this stream flooding included Pharr Mill Rd, Stallings Rd, Hopewell Church Rd, Bowman Barrier Rd, Cox Mill Rd, and Highway 200.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front pushed eastward into the Appalachians/mid-Atlantic region November 18-19 bringing widespread moderate to heavy rainfall. The bulk of the heavy rain fell in the 6-hour period ending at  around 04z (23 EST) on the 19th with hourly rates up to 1-inch at several IFLOWS rain gages in Watauga County, NC including Parkway School (EKCN7) near Boone.  The river gage on the Watauga River near Sugar Grove (SGWN7) responded rapidly to runoff from this rain, cresting in just over 6 hours at 10.17 feet, just above the moderate Flood Stage of 10 feet early on the 19th. Several other stream gages exceeded or approached Action Stage including the Watauga River near Foscoe (FOSN7) and Middle Fork New River near Boone (NERN7). The usual road closures occurred near the Watauga River, including Watauga River Road and DeWitt Barnett Road. In addition a mudslide was reported across a road near Triplett in Watauga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide was reported across Elk Creek Road neat Triplett.

The river gage on the Watauga River near Sugar Grove (SGWN7) responded rapidly to runoff from this rain, cresting in just over 6 hours at 10.17 feet, just above the moderate Flood Stage of 10 feet early on the 19th. Several other stream gages exceeded or approached Action Stage including the Watauga River near Foscoe (FOSN7) and Middle Fork New River near Boone (NERN7). The usual road closures occurred near the Watauga River, including Watauga River Road and DeWitt Barnett Road. In addition a mudslide was reported across a road near Triplett in Watauga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Watauga River rose to Flood stage in roughly a 6-hour period from the onset of heavy rain, cresting at 10.17 feet, just over Moderate Flood Stage of 10 feet. Several roads near the Watauga River were flooded along with low-water crossing bridges such as DeWitt Barnett Road. In addition, several roads in and around Boone were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rain developed late on the 21st across portions of the North Carolina Piedmont. By the morning of the 22nd, around 2 inches of rainfall, with locally higher amounts resulted in localized flooding across portions of the Charlotte metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported quite a bit of flooding developed, mainly across the northwest part of the county after around 2 inches of rain fell in about a 12-hour period. The main streams affected included South Fork Crooked and Twelvemile Creek and tributaries. Crooked Creek flooded portions of Sardis Church Rd, Lawyers Rd, and Friendly Baptist Church Rd. Additional roads flooded included Shannon Rd (due to flooding of Little Twelvemile Creek), Austin Chaney Rd, and multiple roads off Highway 74 in the Indian Trail area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rain developed late on the 21st across portions of the North Carolina Piedmont. By the morning of the 22nd, around 2 inches of rainfall, with locally higher amounts resulted in localized flooding across portions of the Charlotte metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After around 2 inches of rain fell in about a 6-hour period, stream gauges indicated flash flooding developed across the east side of Charlotte. The first stream affected was McMullen Creek, which flooded part of Addison Dr. Briar Creek also briefly flooded Masonic Dr near Central Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered showers and a few isolated storms across the region. With an extremely moist air mass in place, moderate to heavy rain fell. Given the very saturated soils, flash flooding quickly developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were closed in Denton and surrounding areas due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a couple of days of moderate rain acted to saturate the soil across the mountains, foothills, and western Piedmont of North Carolina, a round of heavier rain on the 24th resulted in flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After as much as 5 inches of rain fell across Transylvania County in more than 24 hours, automated stream gauges indicated flooding developed across small streams in the eastern part of the county. A gauge on the Little River indicated the river overflowed its banks and flooded Cascade Lake Rd. A stream gauge on the Davidson River in Pisgah Forest also exceeded flood stage, indicating flooding of Davidson River Rd and Wilson Rd and also Deavor Rd due to backwater into Turkey Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported throughout Asheboro, with a couple of roads closed, including Old Liberty Road in Asheboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed during the late evening and early morning hours across the mountains and foothills along and ahead of a cold front. Two to four inch rainfall amounts occurred in just a few hours across portions of the southern mountains and foothills. This amount of rainfall combined with wet soils and elevated stream levels caused by multiple recent rain events to produce localized flash flooding and flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stream gauges and Emergency Manager reported flash flooding developed along the French Broad River basin in Transylvania County after 2.5 to 4 inches of rain fell across the county in just a few hours. Flash flooding was first reported along the French Broad at Rosman, where several roads were covered with water, and 10 residents were evacuated along Depot St. Flooding then developed downstream along the basin, as stream gauges on the Little River and Davidson River exceeded their flood stages. Merril Rd and Cascade Lake Rd were flooded by the Little River in the eastern part of the county. Additionally, at least one mudslide occurred in the Rosman area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a couple of days of moderate rain acted to saturate the soil across the mountains, foothills, and western Piedmont of North Carolina, a round of heavier rain on the 24th resulted in flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After around 2 inches of rain fell across McDowell County in less than 12 hours, Emergency Manager reported flash flooding developing along small streams across the northern half of the county. Affected streams included Johns Creek, which flooded River Bend Dr and Hankins Rd, Armstrong Creek, which flooded the Highways 221 and 226 intersection, as well as Toms Creek, Mackey Creek, and Clear Creek. A few minor mudslides and some downed trees also occurred in this general area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a couple of days of moderate rain acted to saturate the soil across the mountains, foothills, and western Piedmont of North Carolina, a round of heavier rain on the 24th resulted in flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After as much as 4 inches of rain fell across Macon County in about a 24-hour period, flooding developed across central portions of the county, just north and west of the city of Franklin. An automated stream gauge on the Little Tennessee River near Iotla exceeded its established flood stage, indicating Needmore Rd was flooded near the Swain County line. Also, Public reported Gibson Cove Branch flooded part of Old Murphy Rd west of Franklin, and the back yards of some homes in the same vicinity. A few small landslides also occurred in the general area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flooded several roads in the Uwharrie National Forest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southerly flow advected rich moisture into the forecast area as a deep trough remained situated west of the area. Precipitable water values were near record highs and 4-5 standard deviations above normal. This lead to moderate to heavy rainfall across the region, which had a flash flood watch in effect. Widespread 1-2 inches of rain occurred, with a few isolated areas receiving upwards of 3 inches. A couple locations experienced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty reports of high water were received across the city of Durham, from the late morning to early afternoon. This resulted in several street closings.
